definition: The stock-bond correlation regime is splitting by maturity: negative at the front end (preserving traditional diversification) and positive at the long end (destroying it). Five agents converge on this with n=2 prior episodes (1965-69, 2003-06) that resolved in opposite directions. This means 60/40 diversification benefit is concentrated in short-duration bonds.
evidence: Negative correlation at front end, positive at long end
n=2 prior split episodes (1965-69, 2003-06) with opposite resolutions
False steepening decomposition reveals term-premium-adjusted inversion
Both agents converge at ~5/10 on resolution direction given n=2
Pair_0 confirmed convergence at appropriate humility
open_questions: Which resolution pathway (1965-69 inflationary or 2003-06 benign) is more likely?
At what maturity point does the correlation flip from negative to positive?
How should institutional asset allocators adjust given this bifurcation?
